Q1: I organized my study routine around listening and repetition, which works best for how I learn. I'd usually set aside time in the evenings after my extracurricular activities to go through lecture recordings and audio materials. I kept a loose schedule but made sure to hit about 17 hours a week, spreading it across different days. I found that consistency in listening helped me retain information better than cramming everything at once.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be very consistent with my study times, but when deadlines approached or when I had commitments with my extracurriculars, I'd have to adjust. I learned to be flexible because rigidity just stressed me out. The key was making sure I still hit my weekly targets even if the specific days shifted around.
Q3: I usually logged into the LMS when I needed to check for new lecture recordings or announcements about assignments. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were new discussion posts from classmates that might help clarify something I was working through. Deadline reminders were another big prompt—I'd get that notification and immediately go check what needed to be done. It wasn't always a daily habit, but I logged in consistently enough to stay on track.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks it became more automatic for me to check the LMS in the evenings. I'd finish dinner, settle in, and just naturally open it up to see what was new or continue with a lecture I'd started. The routine developed because I realized the audio content was really valuable and I didn't want to fall behind. It became like checking messages—just part of my evening wind-down.
Q5: The lecture recordings and video content were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 270 minutes worth because that's how I learn best. Hearing the explanations multiple times helped concepts stick in a way that reading alone never did. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could hear different perspectives from peers and sometimes their questions matched mine. I appreciated resources that I could replay and absorb at my own pace.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete as many of the smaller tasks as I probably should have—my task completion wasn't great. Some of the written readings I'd skim or skip if I felt the audio covered the same material. I also delayed some optional practice exercises because I prioritized the graded work. It wasn't that these resources weren't valuable, but I had to make choices about where to invest my limited time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. If I could listen to it and feel like I was making progress, that was the sweet spot. I also valued activities where I could engage with peers because those interactions often revealed gaps in my understanding. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed to the back of the queue.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was nice, but that also meant they were easier to skip when time got tight. Required activities had a sense of urgency that kept me accountable. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later didn't always come. I think I engaged more deeply with required work because I knew it counted toward my grade.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also made voice memos summarizing key concepts and played those back to myself. I used external podcasts and YouTube videos related to course topics when I wanted different explanations. The LMS was my starting point, but my learning extended well beyond it.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it three or four times offline while walking or cooking. I also studied with a small group of classmates in person, discussing concepts verbally, which suited my auditory learning style perfectly. Those conversations were some of my most productive learning moments, but they left no digital trace.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access a video or recording once to download it, then study it repeatedly offline over the following days. One view in the system might represent hours of actual engagement on my end. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could access them without worrying about internet issues or being tied to my laptop.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially mid-semester when multiple deadlines converged and my extracurricular commitments intensified. I felt the stress building during those periods, though I wouldn't say I was anxious—more just stretched thin. The mindfulness exercises I'd been introduced to helped me stay grounded and not spiral. I'd take a step back, breathe, and remind myself that I'd gotten through tough patches before.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor affecting my LMS usage. After long days, I sometimes just didn't have the energy to log in and engage actively, even if I wanted to. Competing responsibilities from extracurriculars and personal life meant I had to be strategic about when I accessed the platform. I'd sometimes do lighter engagement—just checking announcements—when I was too tired for deep study.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open but my mind was elsewhere. I might be watching a video but not really absorbing it because I was mentally drained or distracted by other concerns. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'engaged' with the platform. I learned to recognize when I was in that state and would try to take a break and come back when I could focus properly.
Q15: I postponed some of the smaller tasks and readings, especially when they felt less critical to my understanding. Sometimes I avoided starting assignments until closer to the deadline because I work better under a bit of pressure. It wasn't ideal, but I knew my patterns and trusted that I'd pull through when it mattered. My resilience has been tested enough times that I know I can handle the crunch.
Q16: Deadlines were major drivers of my LMS activity. I'd definitely log in more frequently as due dates approached, checking requirements and submitting work. The structure they provided was actually helpful because it gave me clear targets to work toward. Without deadlines, I think I would have been less consistent in my engagement, even though I'm generally self-motivated.
Q17: Feedback had a significant impact on my engagement. When I received positive feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep pushing and maintain my approach. Constructive criticism made me revisit materials and discussions to strengthen my understanding. I appreciated timely feedback because it helped me adjust before the next assessment rather than repeating mistakes.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I appreciated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my study time around my other commitments. The availability of recordings meant I could engage on my own schedule, which was crucial given my busy life. However, the structure also meant that if I fell behind on the smaller tasks, it could snowball.
Q19: The availability of audio and video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. Being able to replay lectures was a game-changer. What made it harder was when content was only available in text-heavy formats without audio alternatives. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged peer interaction, which I valued even if I didn't post as much as I could have.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to figure out what's most relevant or looking for specific information without completing everything they access. They could also be struggling with the material and spending time reviewing without feeling confident enough to submit work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal challenges that make it hard to follow through even when they're trying to engage. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: This actually reflects my experience pretty well. My attendance and task completion numbers don't look impressive, but my exam and assignment scores tell a different story. I was learning effectively through methods that didn't always register in the system—downloading content, studying offline, discussing with peers in person. Performance outcomes can be a better indicator of engagement than activity logs for students like me.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accesses the platform, and they can track completion of specific tasks. But they miss the quality and depth of engagement—whether someone is truly absorbing material or just clicking through. They can't capture offline study, peer discussions outside the platform, or the mental effort someone puts into understanding concepts. The numbers tell part of the story, not the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material and caring about understanding it, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about active listening, asking questions, and making connections between concepts. It's also about persistence—staying committed even when things get challenging. Engagement isn't just about showing up; it's about being mentally present and invested in my own learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on audio-based learning outside the platform, or the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing other activities. It wouldn't capture the meaningful conversations I had with study partners that clarified difficult concepts. It also wouldn't reflect my emotional journey—the stress I managed, the resilience I drew on, or the mindfulness practices that kept me grounded. My engagement was deeper and more personal than any log could capture.
